macro_segments.title: The status of an itztroblin (millstone frame) as a vessel or attached ground in the context of rainwater making produce susceptible to impurity

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the question: rainwater and the itztroblin frame
Why the itztroblin case deserves separate treatment from the general attachment debate
The Gemara's response: Rabbi Eliezer's position vs. the Rabbis' position
Tosafot's explanation: the itztroblin as a middle category between vessels and ground
Mapping the spectrum: mortar, walls, frames, and vessels
The role of good-faith intent (ayin yafa) in sales vs. other halachic contexts

macro_segments.title: The case of collecting a daughter's dowry from the father's estate, including the itztroblin as landed property

micro_segments.title: Rav Nechemiah's instruction to Rabbah regarding the collection of a daughter's tenth of the estate
Explanation of issur nichsei (tenth of the estate) as a daughter's dowry entitlement
The principle that claims against an estate are collected only from landed property, not movable goods
The conclusion that the itztroblin is considered part of the ground for purposes of collection
Rav Ashi's teaching on collecting dowry from rental income (schar batim)
Tosafot's analysis: when does a daughter's lien attach, and when does rent accrue?

macro_segments.title: The sale of a courtyard and what structures are included in the sale

micro_segments.title: The basic rule: selling a courtyard includes houses, wells, cisterns, and other structures
The exception for separate enterprises like bathhouses and olive presses
Rabbi Eliezer's dissenting view: selling the courtyard means only the air/space, not the houses
The status of sand fields (cholsaot) in the courtyard sale
Stores that are open to the courtyard: whether they are included in the sale based on their openings
Rava's linguistic analysis: the significance of the words dirata vs. darta in determining intent
The parallel to the mishkan's courtyard in the Torah and its inclusive meaning

macro_segments.title: Whether a single act of possession (chazaka) on one plot works to acquire multiple distinct plots with different uses

micro_segments.title: The case of selling a sand field (chulsis) and a pond (metzula) together
Rav Nachman's position: separate chazakot required because the two properties serve different functions
Shmuel's position: ten fields in ten provinces can be acquired by one chazaka if sold together
The Gemara's distinction: connected land serving one purpose versus distinct enterprises with different uses
The resolved position: chazaka on one plot works for the other despite different functions

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbis on attachment to ground
issur nichsei (tenth of estate for dowry)
liens on landed property vs. movable goods
chatzer hamishkan (Torah courtyard of the Tabernacle)
chulsis and metzula (sand field and pond)
